1.description: The mixture model method of estimating the proportion of skilled analysts. This figure illustrates the mixture of skill distributions method. The top panel shows the distribution of true skill as a mixture of skilled and less skilled distributions, where true ability in each component is centered either at μ0or μ1and variation in either distribution is σ0or σ1. The fraction of less-skilled analysts is π0, and the fraction of skilled analysts isπ1=1−π0. The fraction of skilled analysts is the mass of the distribution with ability above zero. Conditional on performance groupj, true ability is normally distributed with meanμjand standard deviationσj. Estimated performance (bottom panel) exhibits additional variation due to estimation error in the performance measure, which results in the more dispersed distributions in the bottom panel. Conditional on performance groupj, estimated ability is normally distributed with meanμjand standard deviationσij=σj2+si2, wheresiis the standard error of analysti's performance measure. The parameter values for this illustration areπ0=0.64,μ0=0.0099,μ1=0.0244,σ0=0.0056,σ1=0.0113, andsi=0.0125∀i. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

2.description: Distributions of recommendation revision abnormal returns. This figure plots distributions of buy-and-hold abnormal returnsABRikfollowing analyst recommendation upgrades (Panel A) and downgrades (Panel B). The plots show abnormal returns resulting from buying upgraded stocks or selling downgraded stocks. The sample contains analysts making at least 20 recommendation revisions from 1993 to 2015. The benchmark return for each event is the market-model benchmark(rf,t+β̂k(rtSPY−rf,t)), where the beta of stockkrelative to SPY (β̂k) is estimated using daily returns over the [−272, −21] trading day window relative to a given event. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

3.description: Cross-sectional distribution of analyst average abnormal returns. This figure plots the cross-sectional distribution of average buy-and-hold abnormal returns (ABR¯i) following analyst recommendation revisions. The sample contains analysts making at least 20 recommendations revisions from 1993 to 2015. Abnormal returns for recommendation revisions are those resulting from buying upgraded stocks and selling downgraded stocks. The benchmark return for each event is the market-model benchmark(rf,t+β̂k(rtSPY−rf,t)), where the beta of stockkrelative to SPY (β̂k) is estimated using daily returns over the[−272,−21]trading-day window relative to a given event.

4.description: Simulated distribution of persistence estimate under mixture model structure. This figure plots the simulated distribution of the coefficient ρ from the regressionABR¯i,t+1=a+ρABR¯i,t+νi,whereABR¯i,t=ωi+ei,tin each time period,ωiis drawn from a mixture of two normal distributions, andei,tis normally distributed, zero-mean noise with standard deviation for analysticorresponding to the empirical standard errorsifor the analyst's average abnormal performance over 1993 to 2004. The mixture distribution from whichωiis drawn is based on the mixture model's estimates of the skill distribution for analysts making at least 20 recommendations from 1993 to 2004. The parameters for the mixture model estimated using this 1993 to 2004 subsample areΘ̂={π̂0,μ̂0,μ̂1,σ̂0,σ̂1}={0.8519,0.0072,0.0217,0.0049,0.0101}. Each simulation creates a panel of two periods for 1,028 analysts with noisy performance measures,ABR¯i,tandABR¯i,t+1, and a resultingρ̂. The plot shows the distribution ofρ̂for 5,000 simulations. The averageρ̂across simulations is 0.22. The dashed vertical line indicates the empirical estimate ofρ̂=0.24from equation (9) when the sample is split into two periods (1993 to 2004 and 2005 to 2015). [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

5.description: Time series of analyst skill. This figure plots the time series of statistics of the estimated cross-section of analyst ability. We estimate a two-component mixture distribution using analyst average performance over 10-year rolling windows. Panel A plots the time series of the cross-sectional mean, first quartile, and third quartile of ability in terms of abnormal return. Panel B shows the estimated fraction of analysts with positive ability. Each data point represents the estimates as of the end of each 10-year period. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]
